result:
The rain came without warning, a sudden hush before the sky split open. I stood at the window, watching the first drops darken the pavement like scattered ink. There’s a kind of magic in the way water transforms a familiar street—blurring edges, softening the stubborn geometry of curbs and fences, turning asphalt into a mirror that holds the gray sky. The world quiets, except for the drumming on the roof, a rhythm that feels ancient and patient.

I thought about how rain carries the memory of oceans, of rivers that carved canyons before any eye was there to witness them. Every drop that falls on my city might have once been a cloud over a distant sea, a tear in the Amazon, a glacier melting slowly into time. It connects things that seem separate. The poet in me wants to believe that the rain falling tonight is the same that fell on the roof of my childhood home, that it carries the echo of my grandmother’s voice humming as she watched the storm from her porch. Memory, like water, has a way of returning when we least expect it.

In the distance, thunder rolled—a low, gentle grumble, not threatening but companionable. Lightning flickered behind the clouds, a soft pulse of white. The air smelled of damp earth and ozone, that clean, electric scent that makes you breathe deeper, as if the world had just been washed new. I opened the window a crack, letting the cool mist touch my face. A few stray drops caught the light from the streetlamp, glowing briefly like tiny falling stars.

Maybe that’s why we love the rain. It forces us to pause, to stand still and listen. For a few moments, there is nothing to fix, nowhere to be. Just the steady, insistent whisper of water telling the earth that it is not forgotten. And as long as the rain falls, all is forgiven.
